one_line_takeaway: TRAIT reframes adversarial-example detection as anomaly detection over the temporal 'loss trajectory' an input traces through a model's saved training-epoch checkpoints, using synthetic loss + LSTM denoising + FFT + Deep-SVDD to hit >97% detection at 1% FRR across image/text/audio and classification/regression without any prior knowledge of the attack.
key_finding: Across every evaluated modality, task, dataset, and architecture, TRAIT achieves AE detection accuracy exceeding 97% (often ~99%) while holding FRR at 1%. It generalizes to regression (>=95-96% at 1% FRR) and to a recent, defense-evasive attack SMACK (USENIX '23) it was never tuned for (95.74% at 1% FRR). Ablation shows both custom components are essential: at 1% FRR on CIFAR10+ResNet18+BIM, removing noise reduction drops accuracy 97.94%->87.43%, removing FFT drops it to 43.20%, and removing both collapses it to 9.32% (FFT/spectrum transformation is the single most important component). It beats the unsupervised baselines NIC and ContraNet on 4 of 5 attacks at a 5% FRR. Under the strongest adaptive attack (attacker uses the defender's own IMs plus a trajectory-similarity constraint), forcing trajectory overlap crushes the attacker's AE success rate to 3.3% (vs 76% normally) yet TRAIT still detects at 95.20% (2.44% online FRR); with surrogate IMs, detection exceeds 99%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TRAIT (Traceable Adversarial Temporal Trajectory Imprints), an unsupervised adversarial-example (AE) detector built on a new observation the authors claim is theirs for the first time: AEs leave discernible TEMPORAL trajectory imprints across the intermediate models (IMs) saved at each training epoch, in contrast to all prior defenses that only exploit SPATIAL/static features of the single deployed model. The core mechanism is a 'synthetic loss' metric: because ground truth is unavailable for incoming inputs at inference, TRAIT uses the target (final) model's own prediction as a surrogate label and computes, for each of the K-1 IMs, the loss between IM_k's output and the final model's output, yielding a per-input loss trajectory of length K-1. This loss metric is deliberately chosen because it is low-dimensional (1 x K) and task/modality-agnostic (works for classification and regression; image, text, audio). Since synthetic-loss trajectories of benign vs. adversarial inputs overlap heavily, TRAIT adds a 'discriminability intensifier': a 2-layer bidirectional LSTM autoencoder for noise suppression, followed by an FFT that maps the trajectory (treated as a time-series signal) into the spectrum/amplitude domain to amplify the difference. Detection is then framed as one-class classification via a Deep-SVDD trained ONLY on benign trajectories, so no prior knowledge of any attack is needed. A preset false-rejection rate (FRR) is a training hyperparameter of the SVDD (the pyod contamination parameter).
threat_model: Attacker: a strong white-box adversary with full knowledge of the target model, unconstrained in compute or perturbation budget (though preferring imperceptible perturbations to preserve semantics), able to run advanced white-box attacks; crucially, in the base setting the attacker cannot access the intermediate models (IMs). Defender: the model deployer, who possesses only the deployed target model and its naturally-generated per-epoch intermediate states, cannot alter training dynamics, and has NO knowledge of the attacker's AE-crafting method. Adaptive-attack setting relaxes this by granting the attacker access to (or the ability to train surrogate) IMs and adding a trajectory-similarity regularizer to evade detection.
limitations: (1) Requires the model owner to store intermediate models, incurring memory overhead; mitigated by showing the first 10 IMs alone (about 90% memory reduction) still give >95% accuracy with <2.5% drop, and by compression/weight-sharing suggestions. (2) Inference latency overhead ~28.5% (sequential loss synthesis 0.004ms + dimension reduction 0.06ms + SVDD 0.07ms vs 0.47ms base per image; IMs assumed run in parallel). (3) The base threat model assumes the attacker cannot access IMs; the adaptive-attack evaluation itself concedes granting IM access is 'out of the capability of an attacker in practice' - i.e., the strongest defeat scenario is somewhat unrealistic, and security rests partly on IMs staying secret. (4) It is a detection-only defense (rejects inputs), does not restore correct predictions, and is applicable only by the party that trained/owns the model, not a third party given just a final model. (5) A detection-accuracy vs. FRR trade-off remains, and softmax imprints outperform the loss metric when available, so the universal-loss choice sacrifices some accuracy for generality.

datasets_used: CIFAR10 (image classification, ResNet18 94.03% / VGG16 93.95% clean accuracy)
CIFAR100 (image classification, VGG16; category-count sensitivity study, 20-100 classes)
TinyImageNet (image classification, ResNet18 67.37% clean accuracy, 200 classes)
AudioMNIST (audio digit recognition, AudioNet 95.40% clean accuracy)
SST-2 / Stanford Sentiment Treebank (text sentiment, BERT 92.43% and RoBERTa+LoRA 92.44% clean accuracy)
New Zealand Land Temperatures / Earth Surface Temperature Data (multivariate time-series regression, Bayesian LSTM, MSE 0.0033)
LibriSpeech + Common Voice (used by the SMACK attack on DeepSpeech 2, WER 10.51%)
quantitative_results: Overall: AE detection accuracy >97% (often ~99%) at 1% preset FRR across all modalities/tasks/architectures
Ablation (CIFAR10+ResNet18+BIM eps=8/255, 1% FRR): full TRAIT 97.94%; remove noise reduction 87.43%; remove FFT 43.20%; remove both 9.32%
SMACK (USENIX '23) on DeepSpeech 2: detection 95.74% / 96.21% / 96.76% at FRR 1% / 3% / 5%; attacker ASR 87.6% (vs SMACK-reported 88.3%)

Adaptive attack with defender IMs: attacker AE success rate falls to 3.3% (distance threshold 0.19) vs 76% (threshold 0.52); TRAIT still detects 95.20% at 2.44% online FRR; with surrogate IMs detection >99% at 1.4% FRR
Scalability: using only first 10 IMs gives ~90% memory reduction with <2.5% accuracy drop, still >95% at 1% FRR (e.g., FGSM 95.88% vs 98.31% full at 1% FRR)
RoBERTa+LoRA (665,858 trainable params = 0.53% of 125,313,028): PWWS 98.55%, HotFlip 98.24% at 1% FRR; storage overhead only ~5.3%
Regression - time-series temperature: FGSM 96.47/96.85/98.23%, BIM 95.46/96.07/96.17% at FRR 1/3/5%
Regression - age estimation: >=96% at 1% FRR across FGSM/BIM/PGD/DeepFool
Latency overhead ~28.5% (0.136ms added vs 0.47ms base per image, single CPU)
Softmax imprint variant lifts FGSM detection from 98.31% to 99.40% at 1% FRR (CIFAR10+ResNet18)
Table 1 examples (CIFAR10+ResNet18, 1% FRR): CW 99.76%, DeepFool 99.59%, JSMA 99.56%, Boundary 99.73%, PGD eps=16/255 lowest at 96.44%
baselines_compared: NIC (Ma et al., NDSS 2019) - unsupervised AE detector, reproduced; PGD detection 96.33% at 5% FRR, worse than TRAIT on the other 4 of 5 attacks
ContraNet (NDSS 2022) - unsupervised AE detector, reproduced; PGD detection 98.40% at 5% FRR (comparable to TRAIT's 98.33%), worse on the other 4 attacks
Implicit baseline: 12 AE attacks used as the detection target - FGSM, PGD, BIM, CW, DeepFool, JSMA, Boundary (image); PWWS, TextBugger, HotFlip, UAT (text); SMACK (audio)
